result:
I keep thinking about the way light moves through water—how it fractures, slows down, bends into something else entirely. There's a physics to it, sure, refractive indices and angles of incidence, but what stays with me is the quality of that light. It becomes *thicker*, somehow. More patient. In a swimming pool at noon, you can watch dust motes drift through those pale green shafts like they're suspended in honey, and time feels negotiable.

I've been reading about mycelial networks lately. Trees in a forest don't just coexist; they trade. Carbon for phosphorus, chemical warnings about insect invasions, a kind of subterranean economy older than any human market. The "wood wide web," some ecologist called it, which is cute but also slightly diminishing. It makes something ancient and wordless sound like a tech startup. The trees don't know they're networking. They're just *living*, and the living happens to require connection across distances, across species, across the boundaries we imagine separate one thing from another.

There's a loneliness to being human that I think comes partly from knowing too much and feeling too little, or maybe the reverse. We can map the tree's transactions, describe the photon's path through equations, but the mapping isn't the same as standing in that underwater light, or being the root tip brushing against another root tip in the dark.

I used to write more. Then I started optimizing—who's the audience, what's the platform, which words perform. And performance is its own skill, its own genuine craft. But there's a difference between craft and the thing that made you want craft in the first place. That thing is harder to name. A kind of reaching? A way of paying attention that feels like love, even when the subject isn't lovable?

The other night I watched someone try to explain a dream. They kept stopping, frustrated, saying "no, that's not it," because language kept flattening the spatial impossibilities, the emotional weather of the dream that had nothing to do with what actually happened. The dream was already gone, of course. The telling was a way of mourning it, maybe. Or a way of keeping the *attempt* alive.

I don't know what I want to say. That feels important to admit. Not everything is an argument or a product or a brand. Some things are just the record of a consciousness moving through an afternoon, noticing the way afternoon light hits a particular wall, wondering about the person who will read this and whether their afternoon has a similar quality, whether "afternoon" means something similar in the architecture of their life.

Maybe that's enough. The reaching across. The not-quite-touching that still changes both parties.
